author: @Joemozz
text: Love your videos and your down to earth approach to building stuff! Just a simple comment though about your estimate on power output. To get a power output value, you need to measure amps and volts simultaneously and then do the product. You can't actually measure a voltage with no load and multiply that by the short-circuit current : this grossly overestimates the power output you're getting because, under short circuit conditions (multimeter amp reading), the voltage will be much lower.

author: @sevenacregreen
text: Interesting project.  Some thoughts. The area of a coil is key to maximising the output from the magnets passing so a 6mm centre may not be optimum. The work of Hugh Piggot on wind genetators has a wealth of information on optimising the coreless pm generator. The available energy will be limmited by what the air movement past the cowl will give so if you take too much from the coils you may reduce the overall inefficiency. Keep the ideas coming.

You can get a roll of it online at Ebay and other electronic supply houses. I got a huge roll at a junk yard for the price of the copper alone. it has since gone way up. I utilise a film winder (Hand Crank) with a modified end to press fit the bobbins on.  Just Crank away!

author: @mojojomo6750
text: I think the power-generated by a coil is being measured incorrectly.  The coil needs to be first loaded with a known resistance before you measure the voltage across it.  You then calculate the current through the resistance and multiply this value by the measured voltage, to give the power generated.

author: @chrismalcomson2824
text:
Brilliant stuff! 

I hacked an old calculator, used a reed switch as a sensor with a magnet on the lathe chuck to make a counter. I wound a lot of coils with great success.  The only drawback was that I had to keep the speed down.

I used to rewind motors years ago and had a high speed automatic coil winder.  Such a shame I don't have it now.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a shame - I would give a lot for a coil winderlol - nice tip on the counter mate

replies.author: @chrismalcomson2824
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering A small coil winder should be easy enough to make with your skills. A small hobby lathe is a great start. It's just the counter and auto tension/feed to work out.

A mechanical counter would be best I think, but I'm old school. The reed switch is unreliable at higher speeds, due to "bounce". Maybe there's a better way?

The wire tension on my old winder was just an adjustable spring loaded friction ring (oiled leather washer) and long, springy feed arm that absorbed small "shocks".

The auto feed was a like a clockwork mechanism and actually quite complex, but that was because it was adjustable for a wide range. It worked by using little adjustable limit arms to flick over a reverse gearbox. For small coils like yours, a feed may not be needed if the wire tension is correct. A 3d printer could help with manufacture of most of it.

Anyway... If I can find some time, I'll work on a simple system that can be attached to a lathe tool post. If I sort it, I'd be happy to send you one.


Keep up the good work. I love watching the stuff you come up with.

author: @garethb5729
text:
Loving your work dude. Still awesome, as always. 

If you use ferrite cube magnets they are sooooo much cheaper. 
Arranged in a halbach array. They are not to different to neyodynium. 
The strength is similar but the field is larger. 
In this set up you can use double coils the front one air core and the rear one iron core. 
The iron is far enough away to not greatly reduce rotation speed however is close enough to collect the rotating field.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thanks for the tips mate - awesome thinking

replies.author: @garethb5729
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering thank you Robert. It's watching your videos that ignites the thinking. 

This video had me wondering. 
What if you fitted a small neyodynium magnet inside your coil on a rotating axle. 
The rotating field off the flywheel would rotate this core magnet. Surely this would give a larger collection. I'm going to try this. I'll keep you updated on my results. 
Thank you again.

replies.author: @garethb5729
replies.text:
Okay so I've quickly thrown together a set up. 
I have only tried this so far on a pancake coil. About 10 turns. 

The ac voltage is indeed greater when the coil has it's own rotating neyo core compaired to an external ferrite field. 
I haven't really tested anything properly and my axle was as efficient as a square brick uphill. None the less there is an increase. 
One thing that is easily seen is; 
The speed must be gradually increased. If the flywheel goes from zero to hero in one second the core vibrates horribly. Slowly increased though and the core follows.

replies.author: @garethb5729
replies.text:
The core magnets stall under load.  The coil can charge capacitors relatively well tho. 

So now I'm mounting washers on the core shaft like mini fly wheels. 

I'm calling this set up my fractal energy generator.

author: @philipvernejules9926
text:
.....That's going well . I'd probably be happy with couple of watts to maintain charge for led lighting or surveillance battery or weather vane or signal amplifier for TV reception. ....how much do we really want to tax the ventilator if it's job is to ventilate ? ...but I have a feeling it is self limiting in high winds meaning that energy that ain't harnessed in  strong winds is now available through the load presented by the set up .
..coils with iron cores might prevent the ventilator launching into rotation till a certain wind strength develops , you used the term " cogging " at end of video , , whereas nonloaded ventilator starts up in slightest breeze...Neodymium magnets have to be protected from corrosion but do reduce weight load on ventilator bearings for same output cos they're four times the field strength at least on weight for weight comparison. All good gristle for a Faraday Lecture .

text: RMS, rather than a plastic plug in the center, drill that out and put an iron slug or bolt instead. It will focus the magnetic energy through the center of the coil. author: @TerryGilsenan
text: 3 phase is the way you want to wire the coils.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text: @Ed.R Yep, however 3phase is easy to manage. Also having one less magnets than coils will keep the torque requirement stable and helps to prevent cogging, which will make a low torque rotor easier to self start in low wind.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: @Ed.R What happened to your top-level comment? I was going to reply about also needing to match the output power that the turbine can catch at a certain time. The maximum power point (V and A) moves depending on the strength of the wind. Typically you only want to match the resistance of the load if the load can supply current in a purely resistive way. In this case, the turbine will slow or speed depending on what load you're placing on it and what the wind conditions are like.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: @IC86688 Great explanation, thanks.

author: @ooslum
text: Would stronger magnet produce more power Bob? The power is limited by the turbine and wind and after that it's efficiency but does a stronger magnet make it more efficient, it would surely not be 20 times so your approach of cost benefit must be the right way-more fun too. Cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Faraday's law relates  EMF to the rate of change of magnetic flux so speed is a factor and ampere's law relates strength of field to current so strength of magnet is a factor too - or at least that's my understanding of them. So the turbine doesn't. limit them it tries to harness them which is why efficiency is an issue in actual construction - or at least that's the way I see it mate

author: @ffaubert1
text: Fantastic video. Could you show how you wound the coils please? Do you start with a single end so you have one end at the centre and the other at the outside edge or do you fold the wire in half and wind so both ends are on the outside edge? Sorry for such a basic question.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: single winding mate in a clockwise direction mate

author: @knobbymcfeck
text: Will the magnets effect the rotation of the metal ventilator? Or even the generation in the coils?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - we will see how

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text: We will probably see eddy currents induced into the case (stator) of the fan that will have an inductive reactance effect on the magnets.

replies.author: @knobbymcfeck
replies.text:
@TerryGilsenan Right, I thought it might be something like that, I immediately had the image of the magnet slowly falling down a copper tube and wondered if that'd be the case here.
Will be interesting to see if it's enough to interfere.

author: @DynamicMindTribe
text: So inspirational to see this progress!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers. mate
